subject: ap-chemistry
theme: Atoms are defined by their nuclear composition, and electron configuration determines their chemical behavior and periodic trends.
core_concepts: The atomic number ($Z$) equals the number of protons; isotopes have the same $Z$ but different mass numbers ($A = Z + N$).
Average atomic mass = $\sum (\text{isotope mass} \times \text{fractional abundance})$; weighted average from all naturally occurring isotopes.

Electrons fill orbitals following three rules: Aufbau principle (lowest energy first), Pauli exclusion principle (no two identical sets of quantum numbers), and Hund's rule (one electron per orbital before pairing).
The four quantum numbers $(n, l, m_l, m_s)$ uniquely specify each electron; $n$ = energy level, $l$ = shape (0=s, 1=p, 2=d, 3=f), $m_l$ = orientation, $m_s$ = spin.
Effective nuclear charge $Z_{\text{eff}} = Z - \sigma$ increases across a period; this drives trends in atomic radius, ionization energy, and electronegativity.
Photoelectron spectroscopy (PES) provides experimental evidence for electron configuration — peaks correspond to ionization energies of different subshells.
Periodic trends: atomic radius decreases left→right across a period; ionization energy increases left→right; electronegativity increases left→right and bottom→top.

key_terms.definition: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons; same $Z$, different $A$
Amount of substance containing $6.022 \times 10^{23}$ particles (Avogadro's number)
Mass in grams per mole; numerically equals atomic/formula mass in amu
Region of space with 90% probability of finding an electron; defined by $n$, $l$, $m_l$
Net positive charge felt by a valence electron after accounting for shielding by inner electrons
Energy to remove the outermost electron from a gaseous atom; increases across a period
Atom's ability to attract shared electrons in a covalent bond; highest in F
Technique measuring ionization energies of electrons in different subshells; provides direct evidence for electron configuration

exam_tip: AP Chemistry frequently tests PES interpretation — the peak with the highest ionization energy corresponds to the innermost (core) electrons, not the valence electrons.
